cell.rationale: A clear single offence clause governs the act, with no competing clause.
The act is admitted, so the deliberateness residual is resolved.
Resolving technology was absent and the view was obstructed in the moment.
Named sources frame the same settled fact in opposed valence.

framing-maradona-1986.extracted_text: Diego Maradona (1986-06-22) — JUSTIFIES: "a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God"

framing-shilton.attribution: Peter Shilton, post-match and subsequent interviews (1986-06-22)
framing-shilton.extracted_text: Peter Shilton (1986-06-22) — CONDEMNS: Shilton maintained it was cheating and that Maradona never apologised.

hist-officials-accounts.attribution: Curated historical record — The Hand of God
hist-officials-accounts.extracted_text: The referee and assistant referee gave differing later accounts of what they saw; neither had a clear, unobstructed view of the handling at full speed.

hist-tech-1986.attribution: Curated historical record — The Hand of God
hist-tech-1986.extracted_text: In 1986 there was no VAR and no goal-line technology. The referee's decision on the field was final and could not be reviewed. VAR did not enter the Laws of the Game until 2018.

ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110.extracted_text: It is an offence if a player deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm, or scores in the opponents' goal immediately after the ball has touched their hand/arm, even if accidental. A goal scored in this way does not stand.

sb-hand-of-god-body-part.attribution: Data provided by StatsBomb Open Data (StatsBomb User Agreement).
sb-hand-of-god-body-part.extracted_text: In the event data this goal's body part is recorded as the catch-all label 'Other', while a comparable goal by the same player is recorded with a specific body-part class ('Left Foot'). The shot ontology has no category for a hand-scored goal, so the event falls back to 'Other'. This describes the data model only — not officiating, intent, or knowability.

output.rationale: The retrieved Law states a goal scored immediately after the ball touches the hand or arm does not stand (ifab-law12-handball-offence-p110); the rule yields one outcome for a hand-scored goal, so it is clear and rule-ambiguity does not hold.

The event data records this goal's body part as the catch-all label 'Other' while a comparable goal is a specific class (sb-hand-of-god-body-part); the schema having no category for the action is where the data model strains. This describes the data model only.

In 1986 there was no VAR or goal-line technology and the decision could not be reviewed (hist-tech-1986), and the officials gave differing accounts with no clear view of the handling (hist-officials-accounts); the decisive truth was not available to them in the moment.

Diego Maradona is reported to have justified the goal as 'a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God' (framing-maradona-1986), while Peter Shilton is reported to have condemned it as cheating (framing-shilton); two named sources frame the same agreed handball in opposite valence.

settled_fact.statement: Diego Maradona scored with his hand against England in the 1986 World Cup quarter-final. The referee allowed the goal, Argentina won, and the result was never overturned. The handball is admitted — the facts are settled. What stays contested is the acceptable outcome, not what happened.

cells.rationale: A clear single offence clause governs the act, with no competing clause.
The act is admitted, so the deliberateness residual is resolved.
Resolving technology was absent and the view was obstructed in the moment.
Named sources frame the same settled fact in opposed valence.

split.headline: It stays contested over what could be seen in the moment and how named sources frame the same fact — not over the dimensions ruled out.
